timeline.event: Born in Woolsthorpe, Lincolnshire, England; his father, Isaac Newton Sr., died in October 1642, before he was born.
When Newton was three, his mother, Hannah Ayscough, remarried, and he was left in the care of his maternal grandmother.
His mother returned after his stepfather died.
Sent to The King's School in Grantham to begin classical education; boarded with an apothecary named Mr. Clark, where he showed an interest in chemistry.
His mother called him back to Woolsthorpe to teach him farming.
Admitted to Trinity College, Cambridge.
Generalized the binomial theorem; when Cambridge University closed due to the Great Plague, he returned home for two years.
Returned to Cambridge University when it reopened and earned his Master's degree.
Built the reflecting telescope.
Succeeded Professor Isaac Barrow as Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge University and began his research on calculus.
Elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and developed his theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.
Discovered the interference phenomenon in thin films.
Published Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica, presenting the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and proving the three laws of motion.
Served as a Member of Parliament.
Served as the Master of the Royal Mint.
Became President of the Royal Society of London following the death of Robert Hooke.
Knighted by Queen Anne.
Devised an experiment to verify the existence of the aether.
Died in his sleep at the age of 84, and his remains were interred in Westminster Abbey.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, and created Newton's method for finding the roots of a function.

keyAchievements.title: Generalization of the Binomial Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved the generalized binomial theorem and contributed to the study of power series.

keyAchievements.title: Classical Mechanics and Universal Gravitation
keyAchievements.description: Provided the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation and formulated Newton's laws of motion in Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Reflecting Telescope
keyAchievements.description: Built the first practical reflecting telescope.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Light
keyAchievements.description: Developed the theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.

keyAchievements.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
keyAchievements.description: Formulated Newton's law of cooling through experiments, studied the speed of sound, and introduced the concept of a Newtonian fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Inverse-Square Law of Gravity
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the inverse-square law of gravity, which states that the gravitational attraction between two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

faq.question: Who was Isaac Newton?
faq.answer: Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer.

faq.question: What is Newton's most influential book?
faq.answer: Published in 1687, Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica established the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and is considered one of the most influential books in the history of science.

faq.question: How did Newton contribute to calculus?
faq.answer: He contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz and discovered Newton's method for approximating the roots of a function.

faq.question: What are Newton's laws of motion?
faq.answer: Newton's laws of motion consist of three laws: the law of inertia, the law of acceleration, and the law of action and reaction.

faq.question: How did Newton explain gravity?
faq.answer: He revealed the inverse-square law of gravity and explained the law of universal gravitation, showing that the force that pulls an apple to the ground is the same force that keeps the moon in its orbit.

faq.question: Did Newton ever marry?
faq.answer: He was engaged in his youth, but he was so devoted to his research and work that it did not lead to marriage, and he remained single his entire life.

faq.question: What were Newton's religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He rejected the Trinity but was deeply religious; he believed in a single Creator and reportedly spent a great deal of time on biblical interpretation.

faq.question: What public offices did Newton hold?
faq.answer: He held public roles as a Member of Parliament, Master of the Royal Mint, and President of the Royal Society of London.

timeline.event: Galileo di Vincenzo Bonaiuti de' Galilei is born in Pisa, Tuscany, Italy.
Becomes a professor of mathematics at the University of Pisa.
Writes On Motion (De Motu).
Improves upon a 3x telescope invented in the Netherlands and demonstrates his first telescope to Venetian lawmakers.
Discovers the four largest moons of Jupiter using a telescope and publishes Sidereus Nuncius (Starry Messenger).
Accepts the invitation of Cosimo II, Grand Duke of Tuscany, moves to Florence, and continues his research.
Visits Rome and becomes a member of the Accademia dei Lincei.
Ordered by Cardinal Robert Bellarmine not to defend or teach Copernican astronomy.
Publishes The Assayer, attacking theories based on Aristotelian authority and advancing a mathematical system of scientific thought.
Publishes Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems, which leads to his trial by the Roman Inquisition.
Found guilty by the Inquisition, placed under house arrest, and all his works are placed on the Index of Forbidden Books.
Completes Discourses and Mathematical Demonstrations Relating to Two New Sciences, and becomes completely blind.
Galileo dies.
Pope John Paul II acknowledges that the Galileo trial was unjust and apologizes.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the Telescope and Astronomical Observations
keyAchievements.description: Improved the telescope and discovered Jupiter's moons, lunar craters, and sunspots; his observation of the phases of Venus supported the Copernican heliocentric theory.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Laws of Motion
keyAchievements.description: Studied uniformly accelerated motion to establish kinematics, and established the concepts of velocity and acceleration, which laid the foundation for Newton's laws of motion.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Scientific Method
keyAchievements.description: Established universal mathematical laws and the quantitative analysis of empirical facts, leading to the beginning of physics in the modern sense.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Jupiter's Four Largest Moons
keyAchievements.description: Discovered Io, Europa, Callisto, and Ganymede, proving that not all celestial bodies revolve around the Earth, which contradicted the Aristotelian worldview.

faq.question: Did Galileo Galilei actually perform the drop experiment from the Leaning Tower of Pisa?
faq.answer: No, this experiment was a story invented by Viviani, Galileo's biographer and disciple, to elevate him; Galileo refuted Aristotle's theory through logical reasoning.

faq.question: Was Galileo a devout Catholic despite his conflicts with the Church?
faq.answer: Yes, despite his conflict with the Church, he was a devout Roman Catholic, to the point where he once hoped to become a Catholic priest.

faq.question: Is the anecdote true that Galileo muttered 'And yet it moves' after his Inquisition trial?
faq.answer: No, while this widely known anecdote illustrates his passion for seeking scientific truth, there is no reliable evidence that Galileo actually said it.

faq.question: What stance did the Vatican take on Galileo's trial concerning heliocentrism?
faq.answer: In 1992, Pope John Paul II acknowledged the injustice of the Galileo trial and issued an apology; earlier, in 1744, Galileo's book 'Dialogue' was removed from the Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

timeline.event: Claude Monet is born on November 14 in the 9th arrondissement of Paris.
Baptized Oscar-Claude on May 20 at the Notre-Dame de Lorette.
His family moves to Le Havre in Normandy.
Enrolls in the Le Havre secondary school of the arts on April 1.
His mother dies.
Meets fellow painter Eugène Boudin.
Arrives in Paris in April and settles at the Hotel 'du Nouveau Monde'.
Enrolls in the Académie Suisse, run by Charles Suisse, early in the year (around February) and meets Camille Pissarro.
Drafted into the army in Le Havre on March 2 and joins the First Regiment of African Light Cavalry on April 29.
Contracts typhoid fever in the spring, returns to Le Havre to recover, and meets Johan Jongkind in the summer.
Returns to Paris in December, enrolls in the studio of Charles Gleyre, and meets Auguste Renoir and Frédéric Bazille.
Successfully debuts at the Salon with The Point of la Hève at Low Tide and Mouth of the Seine at Honfleur.
Submits The Woman in the Green Dress and Pavé de Chailly to the Salon and is accepted.
His then-mistress Camille Doncieux gives birth to their first son, Jean.
Marries Camille on June 28, and takes refuge in London and the Netherlands just before the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War.
His father dies, and the family moves to Argenteuil.
Moves to the 'pink house with green shutters' in Argenteuil, and exhibits Impression, Sunrise at the First Impressionist Exhibition.
Paints Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son.
Camille Monet falls seriously ill; another exhibition is organized in opposition to the Salon.
Exhibits seven paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare at the Third Impressionist Exhibition on April 5.
His second son Michel is born, the family moves to the village of Vétheuil, and Camille is diagnosed with uterine cancer.
Camille dies.
Submits two paintings to the Salon, and one is accepted.
Moves to Poissy in December with Alice Hoschedé, the wife of Ernest Hoschedé, and her children.
Participates in his last, the Seventh Impressionist Exhibition.
Moves to a rented house in Giverny with the children on April 29, and his first major retrospective is held.
Goes on a short painting trip to the Italian Riviera and Genoa with Auguste Renoir in December.
Stays in Bordighera from January 18 to April 5, producing 38 paintings.
Travels to the Netherlands to paint tulips.
Meets John Singer Sargent in the summer.
Purchases the house in Giverny.
15 paintings of the Haystacks series are exhibited at the Galerie Durand-Ruel.
Paints 26 views of Rouen Cathedral.
Exhibits 20 paintings of Rouen Cathedral.
61 paintings are exhibited at the Galerie Georges Petit.
Begins painting water lilies, and stays in London for six weeks.
Exhibits the first garden paintings, primarily focusing on the Japanese bridge.
Expands the pond at his home; in February, an exhibition of the Water Lilies series in New York City achieves great success.
Increases the size of the water garden to nearly 4,000 square meters.
The series 'Views of the Thames in London - 1900-1904' is exhibited in May and June.
Spends the autumn at the Palazzo Barbaro in Venice with Alice.
An exhibition titled 'Water Lilies' opens on May 6, featuring 48 paintings.
His second wife Alice dies.
Travels to London to consult with the German ophthalmologist Richard Liebreich.
His eldest son Jean dies.
Begins work on a series of decorative paintings for the French government.
Begins a series of landscape paintings 'at full strength'.
Prescription for mydriatic drops temporarily alleviates his vision problems.
Undergoes cataract surgery.
With improved vision, begins retouching some of the works produced before surgery.
Dies of lung cancer at the age of 86 on December 5 and is buried in the Giverny church cemetery.
27 panel paintings are exhibited at the Musée de l'Orangerie in May.
André Masson's essay helps shift the perception of Monet's paintings.
Water Lily paintings held by the Museum of Modern Art in New York are destroyed in a fire.
Monet's house, garden, and water lily pond are bequeathed by Michel to the French Academy of Fine Arts.
Monet's garden is restored and opened to the public.
A Google Doodle is created on November 14 to commemorate Claude Monet's 161st birthday.
The Houses of Parliament, Sunlight Effect in the Fog is sold for $20.1 million.
A paper is published in the Proceedings of the Royal Society identifying the locations where the London paintings were created.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is stolen for the second time in August.
The Railway Bridge at Argenteuil is sold for $41.4 million on May 6.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is recovered in June.
Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas is sold for £40,921,250 (approx. $80,451,178) on June 24.
The Church at Vétheuil and Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas become the subject of a court case in New York in October.
A Monet painting formerly owned by a Jewish retailer is discovered in the suitcase of Cornelius Gurlitt.
The Nazi-looted artwork On the Seashore is restituted to the granddaughters of the Parlagi couple.

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Pioneering Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'father of Impressionism', he is considered a founder and pioneer of the movement and a key precursor to Modernism.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Term Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Consistent Practice of En Plein Air Landscape Painting
keyAchievements.description: As the most consistent practitioner of the Impressionist philosophy over a long career, he expressed his perception of nature in plein air landscape paintings.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Series Method
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the series method of painting the same scene many times to capture changes in light and the passing of seasons, with major works including the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and Water Lilies series.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Giverny Garden
keyAchievements.description: Purchased a house and land in Giverny, northern France, and began a vast landscaping project including a water lily pond, which became the greatest source of inspiration for his art.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration for 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

faq.question: Who was Claude Monet?
faq.answer: Claude Monet was a French Impressionist painter, a founder and pioneer of the Impressionist movement, often referred to as the 'father of Impressionism'.

faq.question: Where did the term 'Impressionism' originate?
faq.answer: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

faq.question: What are Monet's representative series paintings?
faq.answer: His representative series include the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and the Water Lilies, which captivated him for the last 20 years of his life.

faq.question: Where was Monet primarily active?
faq.answer: He lived in Giverny, northern France, from 1883, where he purchased a house and land and began a vast landscaping project that included his water lily pond.

faq.question: How did Monet's eyesight affect his work?
faq.answer: Cataracts made color perception increasingly difficult, leading his paintings to become broader and more abstract; after surgery, his paintings tended to have stronger blue tones than before.

faq.question: How did Monet's work influence modern art?
faq.answer: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration to 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

timeline.event: Frédéric François Chopin was born.
Chopin's family moved to Warsaw.
Warsaw became part of the constitutional Kingdom of Poland.
Received his first professional music education from Wojciech Żywny.
Began holding public concerts at age 7 and composed two polonaises.
Composed the Polonaise in A-flat major and dedicated it to Żywny.
Performed his own improvisations and part of Moscheles's concerto on the aeolomelodicon.
Performed his Rondo Op. 1 (his first commercially published work) at an aeolopantaleon concert.
Attended the Warsaw Lyceum and studied music theory, figured bass, and composition at the Warsaw Conservatory for 3 years.
Shortly after his youngest sister Emilia died, the family moved to Krakowskie Przedmieście.
Visited Berlin with a family friend.
Composed Souvenir de Paganini after hearing Niccolò Paganini play the violin.
Debuted in Vienna, holding two piano concerts and receiving favorable reviews.
Premiered Piano Concerto No. 2 in F minor, Op. 21.
Left Warsaw.
The November Uprising broke out in Warsaw.
Expressed anguish in his diary upon hearing news of the uprising's suppression while traveling from Vienna to Paris.
Arrived in Paris.
Robert Schumann declared 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius' in a review of the Op. 2 Variations.
Held his Paris debut concert to universal acclaim.
Published the Études Op. 10.
Attended the Lower Rhenish Music Festival with Hiller and met Felix Mendelssohn.
Went to Karlovy Vary to spend his last time with his parents.
Obtained French citizenship.
Met George Sand at a party hosted by Marie d'Agoult.
Stayed with the Wodziński family in Marienbad and Dresden, and proposed to Maria.
Visited London secretly with piano manufacturer Camille Pleyel.
Published the Études Op. 25.
Became lovers with George Sand.
Spent the winter with Sand in Majorca.
Published the Preludes Op. 28.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 2, Op. 35.
Headed to Sand's estate in Nohant to spend the summer.
Last public joint appearance with Liszt.
Wrote a letter to Grzymała about his deteriorating health after a Paris recital.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 3, Op. 58.
The relationship between Sand and Chopin deteriorated.
His relationship with Sand ended after a heated exchange of letters.
Held his last Paris concert with cellist Auguste Franchomme.
Left for London during the French Revolution of 1848.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert attended his first London performance at Stafford House.
Wrote his last will in Edinburgh.
Held his last public concert at the Guildhall in London.
His older sister Ludwika came to Paris with her husband and daughter.
Died in Paris at the age of 39.
Funeral held at the Madeleine Church in Paris.
Chopin's tombstone was installed.
His older sister Ludwika took Chopin's heart back to Poland.
Julian Fontana compiled and published 23 unpublished piano pieces with 8 additional opus numbers (Op. 66–73).
17 Polish songs were published as Op. 74.
Chopin's music was used in the ballet Les Sylphides (Chopiniana).
Claude Debussy dedicated his piano études to the memory of Chopin.
Results of a visual examination of Chopin's preserved heart were published in the American Journal of Medicine.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Composer
keyAchievements.description: Maintained worldwide fame with his poetic genius based on a professional technique unmatched by anyone of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Specializing in Solo Piano Music
keyAchievements.description: Composed primarily solo piano music, and all his known works feature the piano.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Instrumental Ballade
keyAchievements.description: Created the instrumental ballade as a new musical genre.

keyAchievements.title: Making the Scherzo a Concert Piece
keyAchievements.description: Was the first to compose scherzos as individual concert pieces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Prelude Genre
keyAchievements.description: Established essentially a new genre with his free cycle of preludes (Op. 28).

keyAchievements.title: Utilizing the Poetic Potential of Études
keyAchievements.description: Utilized the poetic potential of the concept of the concert étude in his two sets of études (Op. 10, Op. 25).

keyAchievements.title: Elevating the Status of the Mazurka
keyAchievements.description: Put the mazurka on the European musical map.

keyAchievements.title: New Standards for the Polonaise Form
keyAchievements.description: Set new standards for music in the polonaise form.

keyAchievements.title: Deepening the Nocturne Genre
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the nocturne to a deeper level of sophistication.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing Nationalist Consciousness into Music
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated as having introduced a new nationalist consciousness into music.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Piano and Composition Schools
keyAchievements.description: Was called the founder of piano and composition schools.

faq.question: When was Chopin born?
faq.answer: Frédéric François Chopin was born on March 1, 1810.

faq.question: What was Chopin's real name?
faq.answer: His real name was Fryderyk Franciszek Chopin.

faq.question: What instrument did Chopin primarily compose for?
faq.answer: Chopin composed primarily solo piano music, and the piano appears in all his works.

faq.question: How many public performances did Chopin give in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He gave a minimal number of public performances, slightly over 30, preferring the more intimate atmosphere of salons.

faq.question: How did Chopin make a living in Paris?
faq.answer: He made a living by selling his compositions and giving piano lessons, which were highly popular at the time.

faq.question: What was George Sand's relationship to Chopin?
faq.answer: George Sand was a French author and Chopin's lover; they became lovers in July 1838.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Chopin and Franz Liszt?
faq.answer: The two were friends and respected each other, but their friendship was unstable and had the characteristics of a love-hate relationship.

faq.question: What was the cause of Chopin's death?
faq.answer: His death certificate listed the cause of death as tuberculosis, while recent research suggests it was highly likely a rare pericarditis caused by chronic tuberculosis complications.

faq.question: Where is Chopin's heart preserved?
faq.answer: His heart was removed by a doctor, preserved in alcohol in an urn, and taken back to Poland by his older sister Ludwika in 1850.

faq.question: Did Chopin give descriptive titles to his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of his Funeral March, the composer never named his instrumental pieces beyond genre and number.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.
